macro_segments.title: The Oath (Shvuah) as Preventative Social Control vs. Truth-Seeking Tool

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Gemara's Focus on Why a Shvuah is Required in the Tallis Case
Distinguishing Between Rationalizing (Moreh Heter) and False Oaths; Ben Nanas Case
Sumchos's Position Splitting Without Oath; Objective Basis for Claims
Why Shvuah is Rabbinic and Preventative: Takanah Against Grabbing and Claiming
Shlomo Hamelech Analogy and Prevention of Easy Exploitation

macro_segments.title: Distinguishing Division (Yehaloku) from Escrow (Yehei Munach): The Case of Rabbi Yosi and the Three Hundred Zuz

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yosi's Problem: The Deceiver Loses Nothing Under Split Decision
The Deposit Case: One Hundred, Two Hundred, and the Disputed Third Hundred
Escrow as Leverage to Force Admission; Putting All Three Hundred Away
Testing Whether Rabbanan's Approach Fits: Does Disputable Money Go Into Escrow?
When Split is Possible vs. Escrow: Equal Claims and Theoretically Correct Judgment
Why Rabbi Yosi's Escrow Strategy Should Apply to the Tallis Case
Gemara's Defense: In Tallis Case, Not Obviously Definite Lying (Lo Vadai Ika Ramai)
Second Answer: Escrow Works Only When Liar Loses Something from Escrow

macro_segments.title: The Shopkeeper (Chanvani) and Workers Case: Why Full Payment to Both Parties Rather than Escrow

micro_segments.title: Setting Up the Problem: Conflicting Claims Between Shopkeeper and Workers
Why Not Escrow the Disputed Wages When One Party is Definitely Lying?
Shopkeeper's Defense: The Employer Set Up an Unprotected System
Worker's Counterargument: Not Responsible for Employer's Lack of Witnesses
Resolution: Both Swear and Collect from Employer Due to His Poor Arrangement

macro_segments.title: Decision Tree for Adjudication: Split (Yehaloku) vs. Escrow (Yehei Munach) Based on Possibility of Truth

micro_segments.title: Preliminary Note on Muchzak (Presumption): Physical Possession vs. No Presumption
First Question: Is Split Possibly True? If Yes, Yehaloku; If No, Yehei Munach
Tallis Case as Possibly True (Both Could Have Picked Up Simultaneously)
Deposit Case as Not Possibly True (Only One Hundred Disputed, Clearly Not Joint)
Principle: Split Only When Equal Legitimate Claims; Escrow When Patent False

macro_segments.title: The Shvuah (Oath) in Modeh BeHiktzat (Partial Admission) and Witness Testimony Cases

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Chiya's Braita: Partial Admission Requires Oath on Remainder
Why a Kal Vachomer: Admission as Good as Witnesses Regarding Partial Truth
Potential Objection: Is Oath Really Needed When Partial Admission Occurs?
Gemara's Analogy: Our Mishnah as a Case of Modeh BeHiktzat with Physical Evidence
Strange Comparison: How Holding onto Tallis is Like Witnesses to Partial Ownership

macro_segments.title: Psychology of Rationalizing vs. Brazen Lying: Why Oath Works When Rationalizer Admits

micro_segments.title: Rabba's Principle: People Won't Brazenly Deny Debt to Lender; They Rationalize Instead
Rashi vs. Tosafot on Why Brazenly: Tovah (Kindness) vs. Personal Integrity
The Rationalizer's Psychology: Admitting Part Shows Better Self Willing to Admit All
How Oath Forces Rationalizer to Drop Excuses and Admit (Relevant Before Rosh Hashanah)

macro_segments.title: Challenge to Kal Vachomer: When Witnesses Prove Part, Does Oath Also Apply?

micro_segments.title: The Kal Vachomer Formally Stated: Witnesses Weightier Than Admission So They Bind Oath
Gemara's Objection: Admission Does Not Obligate Payment (Except Debt, Not Fine/Kenas)
Kenas Distinguished: Court-Imposed Fine Requires External Witnesses, Not Self-Admission
Counterexample: Admission Weightier Than Witnesses for Korban (Sacrifice) Requirement
Rabbi Meir's Kal Vachomer on Korban: If Witnesses Can Obligate Execution, So Korban
Rabbis' Objection: Bringing Korban Requires Viduy (Admission of Guilt), Not Mere Fact
Resolution: Rabbi Chiya Aligns with Rabbi Meir—Witnesses Always Weightier
